title: Counting C. elegans Nematodes
author: Cristian Riccio
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.5ung6ve

steps.title: Shake your wormy solution to make...
steps.instruction: Shake your wormy solution to make the concentration of worms in it more homogeneous. Wormy solution Contributed by users

steps.title: Pipet 10 microlitres of wormy solution...
steps.instruction: Pipet 10 microlitres of wormy solution in a streak onto a glass slide. Don't pipet the 10 ul in a blob. That would make it harder to count.

steps.title: From left to right, or right...
steps.instruction: From left to right, or right to left, follow the streak under the microscope and count the worms. For this, you can use a manual click counter to make it easier. You should aim to count between 50 and 150 worms. If the number of worms in 10 ul is higher than this, make a dilution of the wormy solution to achieve between 50 and 150 worms in 10 ul. If the number of worms is less than 50 per 10 ul, consider increasing the volume counted. Click counter Amazon Leica L2 binocular microscope Contributed by users

steps.title: Count another two slides and take the arithmetic mean
steps.instruction: Count another two slides and take the arithmetic mean. You can then infer the concentration of worms for any volume. The inferred concentrations from the three replicates give you an idea about the range within which the true concentration of worms lies.
